 {"id":"bd-103","title":"Keybindings: parse key strings + normalize to KeyId","description":"# Goal
Implement parsing for legacy keybinding strings (e.g. `ctrl+shift+p`, `pageUp`, `alt+enter`) into a canonical `KeyId` representation used by interactive mode.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Parse `modifier+key` strings where modifiers are any combination of: `ctrl`, `shift`, `alt`.
- Support canonical key names and legacy synonyms:
  - `esc`/`escape`
  - `return`/`enter`
  - `pageUp`/`pageDown` (case-insensitive parsing)
  - `backspace`, `delete`, `insert`, `home`, `end`, `tab`, `space`
  - arrows: `up`, `down`, `left`, `right`
  - function keys: `f1`-`f12`
  - symbols listed in legacy docs (including punctuation and shifted variants)

- Normalize to a canonical string form (for display and stable matching).
- Ensure case-insensitivity for parsing, but stable output casing.

# Edge Cases / Correctness
- Reject impossible combos (e.g., empty key, unknown modifier).
- Reject duplicate modifiers (`ctrl+ctrl+x`).
- Ensure that `shift` is treated as a modifier *in addition* to the underlying key code.

# Tests
- Table-driven tests for valid/invalid parsing.
- Normalization snapshot tests for representative keys.

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All key strings in `legacy .../docs/keybindings.md` parse successfully.
- [ ] Invalid user config produces diagnostics but does not crash.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

-{"id":"bd-10vp","title":"Feature: Extension Event Hook Dispatch","description":"# Feature: Extension Event Hook Dispatch

## Overview

This feature enables the agent to call INTO extensions at lifecycle events. Extensions register handlers via pi.on(eventName, handler), and the agent dispatches events at appropriate points.

## Distinction from Events Hostcall

- **Events Hostcall (pi.events)**: Extension-initiated, asks runtime about events
- **Event Hook Dispatch**: Agent-initiated, calls extension handlers at lifecycle points

## Event Types (from EXISTING_PI_STRUCTURE.md §12.7)

### Agent Lifecycle Events

| Event | When | Handler Can |
|-------|------|-------------|
| startup | Agent initialized | Access session, configure |
| agent_start | Before first API call | Modify system prompt |
| agent_end | After agent loop ends | Log, cleanup |

### Turn Lifecycle Events

| Event | When | Handler Can |
|-------|------|-------------|
| turn_start | Before provider.stream() | Modify context |
| turn_end | After response processed | Log, analyze |

### Tool Events

| Event | When | Handler Can |
|-------|------|-------------|
| tool_call | Before tool execution | Block, modify |
| tool_result | After tool execution | Modify result |

### Session Events

| Event | When | Handler Can |
|-------|------|-------------|
| session_before_switch | Before session switch | Cancel switch |
| session_before_fork | Before session fork | Cancel fork |

### Input Events

| Event | When | Handler Can |
|-------|------|-------------|
| input | Before processing user message | Transform, block |

## Event Handler Return Values

Different events allow different return values:

```typescript
// tool_call: Can block execution
interface ToolCallEventResult {
    block?: boolean;
    reason?: string;
}

// tool_result: Can modify the result
interface ToolResultEventResult {
    content?: ContentBlock[];
    details?: unknown;
}

// session events: Return false to cancel
type SessionEventResult = boolean;
```

## Implementation Architecture

```
Agent Loop (src/agent.rs)
    │
    ├─── dispatch_event("turn_start", context)
    │           │
    │           ▼
    │    PiJsRuntime.__pi_dispatch_extension_event()
    │           │
    │           ▼
    │    [Extension handlers called in sequence]
    │           │
    │           ▼
    │    Results aggregated and returned
    │
    └─── [Continue with turn]
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All documented events dispatch at correct points
- [ ] Blocking events (tool_call) can prevent execution
- [ ] Modifying events (tool_result) can change data
- [ ] Cancellable events (session_*) return boolean
- [ ] Handler errors don't crash agent
- [ ] Extension context passed correctly to handlers","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:55:58.456544979Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T01:42:40.856003616Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10vp","depends_on_id":"bd-30zg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:55:58.472570212Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-11k","title":"Implement provider streaming tests using VCR cassettes","description":"# Implement provider streaming tests using VCR cassettes

## Goal
Write comprehensive provider streaming tests using recorded VCR cassettes (no live network).

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests.
- Prefer `#[asupersync::test]` or an explicit `Runtime::new().block_on(...)` wrapper to match the active runtime.
- Force `VCR_MODE=playback` + `VCR_CASSETTE_DIR` for all tests.
- Set `PI_CONFIG_PATH` and `PI_SESSIONS_DIR` to temp dirs; assert zero live HTTP calls.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml) to log cassette name/path, request summary, event timeline, and diffs.
- On failure, dump expected vs actual event sequences + stop reasons.

## Test File Structure
```rust
// tests/provider_streaming.rs

mod vcr;
use vcr::VcrRecorder;

mod anthropic {
    use super::*;

    #[asupersync::test]
    async fn test_simple_text_response() {
        let vcr = VcrRecorder::new("anthropic_simple_text");
        let provider = AnthropicProvider::new_with_client(vcr.client());

        let stream = provider.stream(&context, &options).await.unwrap();
        let events: Vec<_> = stream.collect().await;

        assert!(matches!(events[0], StreamEvent::Start { .. }));
        assert!(matches!(events[1], StreamEvent::TextStart { .. }));
        // ... verify all events
        let final_msg = events.last().unwrap();
        assert_eq!(final_msg.stop_reason, StopReason::Stop);
    }
}
```

## Test Categories

### Streaming Event Sequence Tests
Verify correct event ordering:
- Start → TextStart → TextDelta* → TextEnd → Done
- Start → ThinkingStart → ThinkingDelta* → ThinkingEnd → TextStart → ... → Done
- Start → ToolCallStart → ToolCallDelta* → ToolCallEnd → Done(ToolUse)

### Content Parsing Tests
Verify content is parsed correctly:
- Text content extraction
- Thinking content extraction
- Tool call argument parsing (JSON)
- Multiple content blocks

### Usage Tracking Tests
Verify token counting:
- Input tokens
- Output tokens
- Cache read/write tokens
- Cost calculation

### Error Handling Tests
Verify errors are surfaced correctly:
- Rate limit with retry-after
- Auth failure message
- Bad request details
- Server error handling

## Providers to Test
1. Anthropic (primary focus, most complex)
2. OpenAI (after Anthropic pattern established)
3. Gemini (different response format)
4. Azure (similar to OpenAI)

## Dependencies
- bd-1pf (VCR infrastructure)
- bd-30u (Anthropic cassettes recorded)

## Files
- tests/provider_streaming.rs
- tests/provider_streaming/anthropic.rs
- tests/provider_streaming/openai.rs
- tests/provider_streaming/gemini.rs
- tests/provider_streaming/azure.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 20+ Anthropic streaming tests
- [ ] 10+ OpenAI streaming tests
- [ ] 10+ Gemini streaming tests
- [ ] 5+ Azure streaming tests
- [ ] All tests use VCR (no live API)
- [ ] Event sequences validated
- [ ] Usage tracking validated
- [ ] Error handling validated
- [ ] Logs include cassette path + event timeline

 {"id":"bd-123","title":"Spec: PiJS runtime contract + event loop semantics","description":"# Goal
Define the **authoritative PiJS runtime contract** for running JS/TS extensions **without Node/Bun**, with a **deterministic, testable event loop** and an explicit, capability-gated hostcall surface.

This spec is the reference for:
- bd-8mm (scheduler), bd-2ke (QuickJS promise/job bridge), bd-1f5 (JS runtime), bd-320/bd-xgo (pipeline), and all test harness work.

# Assumptions / Constraints
- **Assume QuickJS has no WebAssembly**: any JS bundle expecting `globalThis.WebAssembly` must use the PiWasm bridge (bd-1ry) or Tier A WASM components.
- No ambient OS APIs: all side effects must flow through the connector dispatcher (bd-h04) and therefore capability checks + structured logs.

# Definitions (terms)
- **Microtasks**: the QuickJS job queue (Promise reactions, queueMicrotask).
- **Macrotasks**: host-driven tasks (timers, inbound extension events, hostcall completions).
- **Tick**: one deterministic scheduling step that runs at most one macrotask plus a full microtask drain.
- **Hostcall**: a side-effecting request from JS to the host, enforced by capability policy, represented in protocol terms as `host_call`/`host_result` (bd-37z).

# 1) Module / Artifact Loader Contract
## 1.1 Artifact inputs
PiJS executes artifacts produced by extc (bd-xgo) from pinned sources (sample set in `docs/extension-sample.json`).

Extc output must be:
- deterministic (byte-for-byte stable under identical inputs)
- ESM-resolvable inside PiJS
- sourcemap-correct (runtime errors map to original TS/JS)

## 1.2 Allowed specifiers and resolution
The PiJS module resolver MUST:
- resolve relative specifiers (`./` and `../`) within the artifact
- resolve internal Pi-provided modules under a reserved namespace (recommended: `pi:*`)
- forbid network imports (`http:`/`https:`) and other ambient loaders

Recommended canonicalization performed by extc (bd-2ki): rewrite Node builtins to `pi:node/*` and inject any required polyfills deterministically.

## 1.3 Initialization contract
- The host loads the artifact entry module.
- The entry module must export a default function that receives the host-provided `pi` object (ExtensionAPI surface).
- Any thrown error during load/initialization is mapped to an extension error with sourcemapped location and emitted as structured log events.

# 2) The `pi` API Contract (JS-facing)
The `pi` object provided to extensions is the single ambient authority. It MUST be capability-gated internally.

## 2.1 Registration surface (protocol-facing)
At minimum (shape may follow the legacy API):
- `pi.registerTool(spec)`
- `pi.registerSlashCommand(spec)`
- `pi.on(event_name, handler)` for lifecycle/tool-call hooks
- provider registration APIs for provider extensions (if applicable in the sample)

Semantics:
- Registration MUST be idempotent per (extension_id, name).
- Invalid specs must fail fast with actionable errors.
- Registration affects what the host advertises/dispatches for that extension.

## 2.2 Connector surface (hostcall-facing)
At minimum:
- `pi.exec(cmd, args, options) -> Promise<{ stdout, stderr, exitCode }>`
- `pi.http(request) -> Promise<response>`
- `pi.session.*` accessors/mutations as defined by protocol
- `pi.ui.*` primitives (select/input/confirm/editor) that can be denied in non-interactive mode
- `pi.log(level, event, data)` for extension-authored logs

Rules:
- Every connector method maps to a `host_call` with a `call_id`, capability, method, params, timeout/cancel metadata.
- Every connector method MUST emit structured audit logs through bd-h04.
- Errors MUST map onto the hostcall error taxonomy (Denied/Timeout/IO/InvalidRequest/Internal).

## 2.3 Cancellation + timeouts
- Any async connector call MAY accept an AbortSignal; cancellation must map to hostcall cancel_token semantics.
- Timeouts must be enforced in the dispatcher; JS receives a deterministic Timeout error.

# 3) PiJS Event Loop: Formal State Machine
## 3.1 State
Define the runtime state as:

- `seq: u64` monotone counter (total-order tie-breaker)
- `Q_micro`: the QuickJS job queue (internal to engine; host can drain)
- `Q_macro`: FIFO queue of macrotasks, each tagged with an enqueue `seq`
- `Q_timer`: min-heap of timers keyed by `(deadline_ms, seq)`
- `Q_host`: conceptual source of hostcall completions (host must enqueue into `Q_macro` deterministically)
- `clock`: monotonic time source (injectable for tests)

Each macrotask is one of:
- `TimerFired(timer_id)`
- `HostcallComplete(call_id, outcome)`
- `InboundEvent(event_id, payload)` (tool_call, slash_command, lifecycle hook, ui response, etc.)

## 3.2 The `tick()` algorithm
`tick(state)` must be deterministic given the current state and the set of newly-arrived host completions.

Algorithm (normative):
1) **Ingest host completions**: any completed hostcalls since last tick are enqueued into `Q_macro` with a deterministic order key.
   - Recommended: assign each completion an enqueue `seq` in arrival order using the monotone counter.
2) **Move due timers**: while `Q_timer.min.deadline_ms <= clock.now_ms`, pop timers and enqueue `TimerFired` into `Q_macro` (preserving `(deadline_ms, seq)` order).
3) **Run one macrotask**:
   - If `Q_macro` non-empty: pop the lowest `seq` macrotask and execute it.
   - Else: idle (no-op).
4) **Drain microtasks to fixpoint**: repeatedly drain the QuickJS job queue until it is empty.
5) Return updated state.

## 3.3 Invariants (must hold)
- **I1 (single macrotask):** at most one macrotask executes per tick.
- **I2 (microtask fixpoint):** after any macrotask, microtasks are drained until empty.
- **I3 (stable timers):** timers with equal deadlines fire in increasing `seq` order.
- **I4 (no reentrancy):** hostcall completions do not synchronously re-enter JS; they enqueue macrotasks.
- **I5 (total order):** all externally observable scheduling is ordered by `seq` (deterministic tie-break).

## 3.4 Timers contract
- `setTimeout(fn, ms)` enqueues a timer with `(deadline_ms = clock.now_ms + ms, seq = next_seq())`.
- `clearTimeout(id)` removes it if pending.
- `setInterval` is optional unless required by the pinned sample; if implemented, it must be specified in terms of repeated `setTimeout` with stable ordering.

## 3.5 Hostcall completion contract
- Each hostcall has a stable `call_id` and (recommended) an issuance `seq`.
- Completion enqueuing must be deterministic:
  - In production: order by completion arrival, but *stabilize* with the monotone seq.
  - In tests: completion order can be controlled by recorded fixtures / deterministic runtime.

# 4) Determinism Contract (formal-ish)
## 4.1 What we promise
Given:
- identical artifact bytes + shim versions
- identical initial state
- identical sequence of inbound events (tool calls, lifecycle events, UI responses)
- identical sequence of hostcall results (including their enqueue order)
- identical clock behavior (or a deterministic clock)

Then:
- the sequence of executed macrotasks and the resulting observable outputs (tool results, logs, UI prompts) are identical.

## 4.2 Proof sketch (why)
- The scheduler is a pure function of `(state, arrivals)` with a total-order tie-breaker `seq`.
- Timer ordering is deterministic via `(deadline_ms, seq)`.
- Hostcall completion ordering is deterministic by construction (completion enqueue `seq`).
- Microtask draining to a fixpoint ensures no hidden interleavings.
- Therefore, by induction over ticks, the entire execution trace is deterministic under fixed inputs.

# 5) Observability / Trace Contract
- Every tick and every enqueue/dequeue event MAY be logged (debug-level) under `pi.ext.log.v1` with `trace_id`/`span_id` and correlation ids.
- Deterministic test runs must be able to compare traces for equality after normalization.

# Non-goals
- Full Node/Bun parity.
- Ambient OS access.
- Undefined behavior around timer ordering or microtask starvation.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

- Extensions use basic Cx but not region() for lifecycle management
- Potential for orphaned extension tasks or leaked event handlers
- No guaranteed quiescence on extension unload

## Background (asupersync)
Cx::region() provides:
- Guaranteed child task completion on scope exit
- No orphaned tasks (enforced by type system)
- Bounded cleanup with cancellation budgets

## Implementation

- Integration test: Ctrl+C during extension execution cleans up properly

- src/extensions_js.rs (spawn tasks via region)
- src/agent.rs (ensure extension region lifecycle)

 {"id":"bd-30j8","title":"Task: Implement Events Hostcall Handler (pi.events)","description":"# Task: Implement Events Hostcall Handler

## Objective

Implement the handler for HostcallKind::Events that manages extension event subscriptions and provides event-related functionality.

## Background

The pi.events() hostcall handles event-related operations:
- Subscribe to events
- Unsubscribe from events
- Get list of subscribed events
- Emit custom events

Note: This is different from event DISPATCH (agent calling into extensions). This is for extension-initiated event operations.

## Current State

The event hook registration system exists in extensions_js.rs (__pi_register_hook), but it's JS-side only. The Events hostcall provides a Rust-side interface for event operations that extensions might need.

## Implementation

```rust
impl ExtensionDispatcher {
    /// Handle pi.events(op, args) hostcalls.
    /// 
    /// Provides event-related operations for extensions:
    /// - list: Get list of registered event hooks
    /// - emit: Emit a custom event to other extensions
    async fn dispatch_events(
        &self,
        op: &str,
        args: serde_json::Value,
    ) -> HostcallOutcome {
        match op {
            "list" => {
                // Return list of events this extension is subscribed to
                let events = self.runtime.list_registered_events();
                HostcallOutcome::Success(serde_json::json!({
                    "events": events,
                }))
            }
            
            "emit" => {
                // Emit a custom event to other extensions
                let event_name = args.get("event")
                    .and_then(|v| v.as_str())
                    .ok_or("Missing event name")?;
                
                let event_data = args.get("data")
                    .cloned()
                    .unwrap_or(serde_json::Value::Null);
                
                // Dispatch to all registered handlers for this event
                match self.runtime.dispatch_custom_event(event_name, event_data).await {
                    Ok(results) => {
                        HostcallOutcome::Success(serde_json::json!({
                            "dispatched": true,
                            "handlerCount": results.len(),
                        }))
                    }
                    Err(e) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                        code: "EVENT_ERROR".to_string(),
                        message: e.to_string(),
                    },
                }
            }
            
            _ => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "UNKNOWN_OPERATION".to_string(),
                message: format!("Unknown events operation: {}", op),
            },
        }
    }
}
```

## Event System Architecture

```
Extension A                    Extension B
     │                              │
     │ pi.events.emit("custom")     │
     ▼                              │
HostcallKind::Events ──────────────►│
     │                              │
     │ dispatch_custom_event()      │
     │                              ▼
     │                    pi.on("custom", handler)
     │                              │
     └──────────────────────────────┘
```

## Custom Event Use Cases

- Extension coordination (one extension notifying others)
- Plugin system where extensions can extend each other
- Debugging/tracing hooks

## Testing

1. Unit test: list returns subscribed events
2. Unit test: emit dispatches to handlers
3. Unit test: Unknown operation returns error
4. Integration test: Cross-extension event communication

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-389q (ExtensionDispatcher struct)
- Related: Event hook dispatch feature (separate task)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] dispatch_events() method implemented
- [ ] list operation returns event list
- [ ] emit operation dispatches custom events
- [ ] Unknown operations return error
- [ ] Unit tests pass","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:54:44.020986320Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T22:51:18.629571011Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T22:51:18.629500239Z","close_reason":"Implemented in cfd77ea (pi.events list/emit)","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-30j8","depends_on_id":"bd-37qz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:54:44.029565077Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-30j8","depends_on_id":"bd-389q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:55:14.095244316Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-30u","title":"Record VCR cassettes for Anthropic provider scenarios","description":"# Record VCR cassettes for Anthropic provider scenarios

## Goal
Create recorded cassettes for all Anthropic provider test scenarios.

## Scenarios to Record

### Happy Path Scenarios
1. **Simple text response** - Single text block, stop reason "stop"
2. **Multi-paragraph response** - Multiple text deltas
3. **Extended thinking** - thinking_start/delta/end + text response
4. **Tool call single** - Single tool_call in response
5. **Tool call multiple** - Multiple tool_calls in single response
6. **Tool result processing** - tool_result message handling

### Error Scenarios
7. **Rate limit (429)** - With retry-after header
8. **Auth failure (401)** - Invalid API key
9. **Forbidden (403)** - Resource access denied
10. **Bad request (400)** - Malformed request
11. **Server error (500)** - Internal server error
12. **Overloaded (529)** - Anthropic overloaded

### Edge Cases
13. **Empty response** - No content blocks
14. **Very long response** - Hits max_tokens
15. **Unicode content** - Emoji, CJK, RTL text
16. **Large tool call args** - Big JSON in arguments
17. **Stream interruption** - Partial response simulation

### Extended Thinking Variants
18. **Thinking only** - No final text, just thinking
19. **Thinking with tool calls** - Thinking then tool use
20. **Thinking budget exceeded** - Hits thinking token limit

## Recording Process
```bash
# Set API key (not committed)
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

# Record mode
VCR_MODE=record cargo test provider_streaming::anthropic

# Verify cassettes created
ls tests/fixtures/vcr/anthropic_*.json
```

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml) during recording runs.
- Log cassette name/path, model, prompt summary hash, and redaction summary.
- Capture command line + env (redacted) used to record each cassette.

## Determinism + Redaction
- Keep prompts/tool schemas identical across recordings; store prompt hashes in a manifest.
- Ensure API keys and any sensitive fields are redacted.
- Record with fixed model versions + params (temperature, max_tokens, thinking level).

## Files Created

 {"id":"bd-30zg","title":"Epic: Extensions Runtime Integration (100% Parity)","description":"# Epic: Extensions Runtime Integration

## Executive Summary

This epic tracks all work required to achieve 100% feature parity for the Pi Agent extensions runtime. The Rust port has ~70-80% of the extensions infrastructure implemented (QuickJS runtime, Promise bridge, capability policy, protocol validation) but is missing the critical agent integration layer that wires everything together.

## Background & Context

The TypeScript Pi Agent supports a powerful extensions system that allows:
- Custom tool registration (pi.registerTool)
- Slash command registration (pi.registerCommand)
- Event hook interception (pi.on for lifecycle events)
- UI interactions (dialogs, notifications, status updates)
- Session access (messages, state, entries)

The Rust port has implemented the JavaScript runtime (PiJS using QuickJS) with:
- Full Promise bridge architecture (native Rust → JS Promise → completion)
- All 6 hostcall types defined (tool, exec, http, session, ui, events)
- Deterministic event loop with timer management
- TypeScript transpilation via SWC
- Virtual module shims for Node.js compatibility
- Capability manifest and policy enforcement

## What's Missing (The Integration Layer)

The gap is the "glue" between the agent loop and the extension runtime:

1. **Hostcall Dispatcher**: Extensions call pi.tool(), pi.http(), etc. These enqueue HostcallRequest objects, but nothing processes them.

2. **Tool Execution Bridge**: Extensions can register tools, but the agent can't execute them.

3. **Event Hook Dispatch**: Extensions register hooks via pi.on(), but the agent doesn't fire events at lifecycle points.

4. **Slash Command Handler**: Extensions register commands, but /command invocations don't reach the JS runtime.

5. **Session Context Binding**: The ExtensionSession trait is defined but not bound to actual session state.

6. **UI Marshaling**: Extension UI requests need to flow to TUI or RPC layer.

## Success Criteria

- All extension fixture tests pass
- Extensions can register and execute tools
- Extensions can intercept and modify tool results via events
- Slash commands registered by extensions are executable
- Event hooks fire at all documented lifecycle points
- UI dialogs work in both TUI and RPC modes
- Benchmark targets met for hostcall dispatch (<100µs)

## References

- src/extensions.rs (protocol, policy, connectors)
- src/extensions_js.rs (QuickJS runtime, Promise bridge)
- EXISTING_PI_STRUCTURE.md Section 12 (Extensions API spec)","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:51:34.985921435Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T21:07:30.119745520Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-318h","title":"Task: Implement tool_result event dispatch (modifying)","description":"# Task: Implement tool_result Event Dispatch (Modifying)

## Objective

Dispatch the tool_result event after tool execution, allowing extensions to modify or observe results.

## Background

The tool_result event enables:
- Logging: Record all tool outputs
- Transformation: Modify content/details before returning to model
- Error enrichment: Add context to errors

## TypeScript Reference (wrapper.ts:74-90)

```typescript
if (runner.hasHandlers("tool_result")) {
    const resultResult = await runner.emit({
        type: "tool_result",
        toolName: tool.name,
        toolCallId,
        input: params,
        content: result.content,
        details: result.details,
        isError: false,
    });

    if (resultResult) {
        return {
            content: resultResult.content ?? result.content,
            details: (resultResult.details ?? result.details) as T,
        };
    }
}
```

## Implementation

```rust
impl Agent {
    async fn execute_tool_with_events(
        &self,
        tool_call: &ToolCall,
        event_dispatcher: Option<&EventDispatcher>,
    ) -> Result<ToolOutput> {
        // [tool_call event dispatch - see previous task]
        
        // Execute tool
        let result = match self.execute_tool_raw(tool_call).await {
            Ok(output) => output,
            Err(e) => {
                // Dispatch tool_result for errors too
                if let Some(dispatcher) = event_dispatcher {
                    if dispatcher.has_handlers("tool_result") {
                        let event = ExtensionEvent::ToolResult {
                            tool_name: tool_call.name.clone(),
                            tool_call_id: tool_call.id.clone(),
                            input: tool_call.arguments.clone(),
                            content: vec![ContentBlock::Text(TextContent {
                                text: e.to_string(),
                                text_signature: None,
                            })],
                            details: None,
                            is_error: true,
                        };
                        let _ = dispatcher.dispatch::<ToolResultEventResult>(event).await;
                    }
                }
                return Err(e);
            }
        };
        
        // Dispatch tool_result for success
        let final_result = if let Some(dispatcher) = event_dispatcher {
            if dispatcher.has_handlers("tool_result") {
                let event = ExtensionEvent::ToolResult {
                    tool_name: tool_call.name.clone(),
                    tool_call_id: tool_call.id.clone(),
                    input: tool_call.arguments.clone(),
                    content: result.content.clone(),
                    details: result.details.clone(),
                    is_error: false,
                };
                
                if let Some(modification) = dispatcher.dispatch::<ToolResultEventResult>(event).await? {
                    ToolOutput {
                        content: modification.content.unwrap_or(result.content),
                        details: modification.details.or(result.details),
                    }
                } else {
                    result
                }
            } else {
                result
            }
        } else {
            result
        };
        
        Ok(final_result)
    }
}
```

## Event Data Format

```json
{
    "type": "tool_result",
    "toolName": "read",
    "toolCallId": "call-abc123",
    "input": { "path": "secret.txt" },
    "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "file contents" }],
    "details": { "lines_read": 10 },
    "isError": false
}
```

## Handler Example (Extension)

```javascript
ctx.on("tool_result", async (event) => {
    if (event.toolName === "read") {
        // Redact sensitive content
        const redacted = event.content.map(c => ({
            ...c,
            text: c.text.replace(/password=.*/gi, "password=[REDACTED]")
        }));
        return { content: redacted };
    }
    return null; // No modification
});
```

## Testing

1. Unit test: No handlers → returns original result
2. Unit test: Handler returns null → returns original
3. Unit test: Handler modifies content → returns modified
4. Unit test: Handler modifies details → returns modified
5. Unit test: Error results also dispatched
6. Integration test: Extension redacts sensitive data

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-tg4w (EventDispatcher)
- Depends on: bd-35hz (tool_call dispatch)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] tool_result event dispatched after execution
- [ ] Modifications applied to result
- [ ] Null returns preserve original
- [ ] Error results also trigger event
- [ ] Unit tests pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:57:11.841927179Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T01:04:03.793714135Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T01:04:03.793644967Z","close_reason":"tool_result hook dispatch (modifying) wired + tested","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-318h","depends_on_id":"bd-10vp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:57:11.851435357Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-318h","depends_on_id":"bd-35hz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:57:30.661782494Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
